subject: Chemistry
q: Which one of the following is an example of disproportionation reaction?
question: Which one of the following is an example of disproportionation reaction?
solution:
Disproportionation reaction, also sometimes called dismutation reaction, is basically a type of redox reaction involving simultaneous reduction and oxidation of atoms of the same element from one oxidation state (OS) to two different oxidation states.

3MnO42-+6+4H+⟶2MnO4-+7+MnO2+4+2H2O

subject: Chemistry
q: 500 mL of 1.2 M KI solution is mixed with 500 mL of $0.2 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ solution in basic medium. The liberated iodine was titrated with standard $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ solution in the presence of starch indicator till the blue color disappeared. The volume (in L) of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ consumed is $\_\_\_\_$. (Nearest integer)
question: 500 mL of 1.2 M KI solution is mixed with 500 mL of $0.2 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ solution in basic medium. The liberated iodine was titrated with standard $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ solution in the presence of starch indicator till the blue color disappeared. The volume (in L) of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ consumed is $\_\_\_\_$. (Nearest integer)
solution:
$MnO_4^- + I^- \rightarrow MnO_2 + I_2$
$I_2 + S_2O_3^{2-} \rightarrow S_4O_6^{2-} + I^-$
gram eq of $KMnO_4$ = gram eq of $Na_2S_2O_3$
$0.2 \times \dfrac{500}{1000} \times 3 = 0.1 \times V \times 1$
$V = 3 \text{ L}$

subject: Chemistry
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><msub><mi>MKMnO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> solution was added to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>05</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><mi>Mohr</mi></math> 's salt solution through a burette. The volume of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>KMnO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> solution left in the burette after the end point is....... <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mL</mi></math>. (nearest integer)
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><msub><mi>MKMnO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> solution was added to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>05</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><mi>Mohr</mi></math> 's salt solution through a burette. The initial reading of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> burette is zero. The volume of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>KMnO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> solution left in the burette after the end point is....... <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mL</mi></math>. (nearest integer)
solution:
Fe2++MnO4-⟶Fe3++Mn2+

vf=1  vf=5

vf=Valency factor

Milli equivalents of mohr's salt = milli equivalents of KMnO4

1×0.05×20=5 0.01×Vml

Vml =20ml

Volume of KMnO4 left = 50-20=30ml

subject: Chemistry
q: The normality of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> in the solution obtained on mixing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><mi>NaOH</mi></math> is____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>.
question: The normality of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> in the solution obtained on mixing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><mi>NaOH</mi></math> is____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>.
solution:
No. of equivalents of H2SO4=M×V×n-factor = 100×0.1×2=20

No. of equivalents of NaOH=50×0.1=5

No. of equivalents of H2SO4 left =20-5=15

⇒150×x=15

x=110=0.1 N=1×10-1 N

subject: Chemistry
q:
(A) HOCl+H2O2→H3O++Cl-+O2

(B) I2+H2O2+2OH-→2I-+2H2O+O2

Choose the correct option.
question:
(A) HOCl+H2O2→H3O++Cl-+O2

(B) I2+H2O2+2OH-→2I-+2H2O+O2

Choose the correct option.
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> act as oxidizing and reducing agent respectively in equations <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>)</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>.</mo></math>

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> acts as oxidising agent in equations <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>)</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>.</mo></math>

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> acts as reducing and oxidising agent respectively in equations <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>)</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>.</mo></math>

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> acts as reducing agent in equations <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>)</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
In (A) reduction of HOCl occurs, so it will be an oxidising agent hence H2O2 will be a reducing agent.

In (B) reduction of I2 occurs, so it will be an oxidising agent and H2O2 will be a reducing agent.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider titration of NaOH solution versus 1.25 M oxalic acid solution. At the end point following burette readings were obtained.
(i) 4.5 mL
(ii) 4.5 mL
(iii) 4.4 mL
(iv) 4.4 mL
(v) 4.4 mL
If the volume of oxalic acid taken was 10.0 mL then the molarity of the NaOH solution is ___M. (Rounded-off to the nearest integer)
question:
Consider titration of NaOH solution versus 1.25 M oxalic acid solution. At the end point following burette readings were obtained.
(i) 4.5 mL
(ii) 4.5 mL
(iii) 4.4 mL
(iv) 4.4 mL
(v) 4.4 mL
If the volume of oxalic acid taken was 10.0 mL then the molarity of the NaOH solution is ___M. (Rounded-off to the nearest integer)
solution:
VNaOH=4.4 ml

eq. of NaOH= eq. of H2C2O4

or, M×4.4×1=1.25×10×2

or, M=5.68M

∴ Nearest integer answer is 6.

q: The oxidation states of nitrogen in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NO</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>NO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mi>NO</mi><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo></msubsup></math> are in the order of :
question: The oxidation states of nitrogen in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NO</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>NO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mi>NO</mi><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo></msubsup></math> are in the order of :
solution:
The oxidation states of Nitrogen in following molecules are as follows

NO3-→+5

NO2→+4

NO→+2

N2O→+1

subject: Chemistry
q:
In the following balanced reaction,
values of $X, Y$ and $Z$ respectively are
question:
In the following balanced reaction,
values of $X, Y$ and $Z$ respectively are
solution:
$X \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}+Y \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{-}+Z \mathrm{H}^{+} \leftrightharpoons$
$$
X \mathrm{Mn}^{++}+2 Y \mathrm{CO}_2+\frac{Z}{2} \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}
$$
First half reaction
$$
\mathrm{MnO}_4^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{++}
$$
On balancing
$$
\mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}+8 \mathrm{H}^{+}+5 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{++}+4 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}
$$


Second half reaction
$$
\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_2
$$
On balancing
$$
\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_2+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}
$$
On multiplying eqn. (ii) by 5 and (iv) by 2 and then adding we get
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 2 \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}+5 \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{--}+16 \mathrm{H}^{+} \longrightarrow \\
& 2 \mathrm{Mn}^{++}+10 \mathrm{CO}_2+8 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Chemistry
q:
For the reactions,
$\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_2 ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-393 \mathrm{~J}$
$2 \mathrm{Zn}+\mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{ZnO} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-412 \mathrm{~J}$
question:
For the reactions,
$\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_2 ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-393 \mathrm{~J}$
$2 \mathrm{Zn}+\mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{ZnO} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-412 \mathrm{~J}$
options: oxidation of carbon is not feasible
oxidation of Zn is not feasible
solution: $\Delta \mathrm{H}$ negative shows that the reaction is spontaneous. Higher value for $\mathrm{Zn}$ shows that the reaction is more feasible.

solution:
Cr2O72-+OH-⟶CrO42-

subject: Chemistry
q: In alkaline medium, the reduction of permanganate anion involves a gain of ____________ electrons.
question: In alkaline medium, the reduction of permanganate anion involves a gain of ____________ electrons.
solution:
In Faintly alkaline medium potassium permanganate changes to manganese dioxide in its oxidising action.

MnO4-+2H2O+3e-→MnO2+4OH-

The oxidation state of manganese in MnO4- is +7 and in MnO2 is +4.

Hence, it transfers 3 electrons during the oxidising action in alkaline medium.

subject: Chemistry
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> equimolar mixture of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Na</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>xg</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>yg</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Na</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>.</mo></math> (Nearest integer)
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> equimolar mixture of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Na</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>xg</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>yg</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Na</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>.</mo></math> (Nearest integer)
solution:
Total mass=4g

Now

NaOH: a mol WNaOH+WNa2CO3=4

Na2CO3: 'a' mol ⇒40a+106a=4

⇒a=4146 mol

⇒ therefore mass of NaOH is: 4146×40 g

=1.095≈1

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: In redox titration, the indicators used are sensitive to change in pH of the solution.
Statement II: In acid-base titration, the indicators used are sensitive to change in oxidation potential.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: In redox titration, the indicators used are sensitive to change in pH of the solution.
Statement II: In acid-base titration, the indicators used are sensitive to change in oxidation potential.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
options: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
solution:
In redox titrations, the indicator changes color when the titration reaches the equivalence point, which corresponds to the point at which the reducing agent has been completely oxidized or the oxidizing agent has been completely reduced. So these are used to change in oxidation potential and not change in pH.
In acid base titration, the indicators are sensitive to change in pH and not change in oxidation potential.
∴ Both the statements are incorrect.

subject: Chemistry
q: The oxidation states of transition metal atoms in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>Cr</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>7</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>KMnO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>FeO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mo>,</mo></math> respectively, are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> . The sum of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> is______
question: The oxidation states of transition metal atoms in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>Cr</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>7</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>KMnO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>FeO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mo>,</mo></math> respectively, are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> . The sum of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> is______
solution:
Compound  Oxidation state of transition element.

Oxidation states of K =+1 and O=-2

(i) K2Cr2O7     x=+6
(ii) KMnO4          y=+7
(iii) K2FeO4        z=+6
so (x+y+z)=19

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the given reactions is not an example of disproportionation reaction?
question: Which of the given reactions is not an example of disproportionation reaction?
solution:
Disproportionation reaction, is a type of redox reaction involving simultaneous reduction and oxidation of atoms of the same element from one oxidation state (OS) to two different oxidation states.

2H2O2-1⟶2H2O-2+O20 : Disproportionation

2NO2+H2O→HNO3+HNO2 : Disproportionation

MnO4-+4H++3e-→MnO2+2H2O : reduction only

3MnO42-+4H+→2MnO4-+MnO2+4+2H2O : Disproportionation

subject: Chemistry
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>KMnO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> decomposes on heating at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>513</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> to form <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> along with
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>KMnO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> decomposes on heating at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>513</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> to form <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> along with
solution:
Potassium permanganate decomposes when heated, the oxidation state of manganese changes as the oxidation state of KMnO4 is +7 decomposes to potassium manganate which oxidation state is +6 and manganese dioxide its oxidation state is +4.

It is a redox reaction in which Mn which undergo both oxidation and reduction such type of reaction is known as a disproportionation reaction.

2KMnO4→513 KK2MnO4+MnO2+O2

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements: One is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A : Permanganate titration are not performed in presence of hydrochloric acid.

Reason R : Chlorine is formed as a consequence of oxidation of hydrochloric acid.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements: One is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A : Permanganate titration are not performed in presence of hydrochloric acid.

Reason R : Chlorine is formed as a consequence of oxidation of hydrochloric acid.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> are true and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> are true but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is NOT the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is true but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is false
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is false but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is true
solution:
Hydrochloric acid (HCl) is usually not used in the process of titration because it reacts with the indicator potassium permanganate (KMnO4) that is used in the process. It reacts with KMnO4 solution and gets oxidized which further results in the liberation of chlorine gas according to the following reaction.

2KMnO4+16HCl→2MnCl2+2KCl+8H2O+5Cl2

So, Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A

subject: Chemistry
options: Bromine is a stronger oxidant than iodine
Thiosulphate undergoes oxidation by bromine and reduction by iodine in these reaction
Bromine is a weaker oxidant than iodine
Bromine undergoes oxidation and iodine undergoes reduction in these reactions
solution:
In the reaction of $\mathrm{S}_2 \mathrm{O}_3{ }^{2-}$ with $\mathrm{I}_2$, oxidation state of sulphur changes to +2 to +2.5
In the reaction of $\mathrm{S}_2 \mathrm{O}_3{ }^{2-}$ with $\mathrm{Br}_2$, oxidation state of sulphur changes from +2 to +6 .
$\therefore$ Both $\mathrm{I}_2$ and $\mathrm{Br}_2$ are oxidant (oxidising agent) and $\mathrm{Br}_2$ is stronger oxidant than $\mathrm{I}_2$.

subject: Chemistry
q:
A 2.0 g sample containing MnO2 is treated with HCl liberating Cl2. The Cl2 gas is passed into a solution of KI and 60.0mL of 0.1MNaS2O3 is required to titrate the liberated iodine. The percentage of MnO2 in the sample is____. Nearest integer)
[Atomic masses (in u) Mn=55;Cl=35.5:O=16,I=127,Na=23,K=39,S=32]
question:

[Atomic masses (in u) Mn=55;Cl=35.5:O=16,I=127,Na=23,K=39,S=32]
solution:
MnO2+4HCl⟶MnCl2+Cl2g+2H2O

Cl2+2Kl⟶I2+2KCl

I2+2Na2 S2O3⟶2Nal+Na2 S4O6

Mili eq. of MnO2= Mili eq. of Cl2= Mili eq. of I2= Mili eq. of Hypo.

2w87=0.1×60

W=261 miligram

% of MnO2=0.2612×100=13.05%≈13

subject: Chemistry
q: An example of a disproportionation reaction is
question: An example of a disproportionation reaction is
options: <math><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mo>→</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></math>

<math><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mo>+</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>→</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mo>+</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math>
solution:
Disproportionation reaction: In this type of reaction, same elemental species gets oxidised as well as reduced.

 Na+1Br-1+Cl20→2Na+1Cl-1+Br20-not disproportionate,

2KMn+7O4→K2Mn+6O4+Mn+4O2+O2-not disproportionate,

2Mn+7O4-+10J-+16H+→2Mn+2+SI2+8H2O-not disproportionate
 

-It is disproportionate.

subject: Chemistry
q: The volume of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> aqueous <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HBr</mi></math> required to neutralize <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> aqueous <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Ba</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>OH</mi><msub><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is (Assume complete neutralization)
question: The volume of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> aqueous <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HBr</mi></math> required to neutralize <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> aqueous <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Ba</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>OH</mi><msub><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is (Assume complete neutralization)
solution:
The balanced chemical equation for the reaction between HBr and Ba(OH)2 is:

2HBr+Ba(OH)2 → BaBr2+2H2O

From this equation, we can see that 2 moles of HBr react with 1 mole of Ba(OH)2.

Equal equivalents will react. 

Number of equivalents = Normality ×Volume

So,

N1V1=N2V20.02×V1=0.02×10V1=10 ml

subject: Chemistry
q: Only \(2 \mathrm{~mL}\) of \(\mathrm{KMnO}_4\) solution of unknown molarity is required to reach the end point of a titration of \(20 \mathrm{~mL}\) of oxalic acid \((2 \mathrm{M})\) in acidic medium. The molarity of \(\mathrm{KMnO}_4\) solution should be \(\qquad\) M.
question: Only \(2 \mathrm{~mL}\) of \(\mathrm{KMnO}_4\) solution of unknown molarity is required to reach the end point of a titration of \(20 \mathrm{~mL}\) of oxalic acid \((2 \mathrm{M})\) in acidic medium. The molarity of \(\mathrm{KMnO}_4\) solution should be \(\qquad\) M.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { eq. }\left(\mathrm{KMnO}_4\right)=\text { eq. }\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right) \\ & \mathrm{M} \times 2 \times 5=2 \times 20 \times 2 \\ & \mathrm{M}=8 \mathrm{M}\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I) : Potassium hydrogen phthalate is a primary standard for standardisation of sodium hydroxide solution.
Statement (II) : In this titration phenolphthalein can be used as indicator.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I) : Potassium hydrogen phthalate is a primary standard for standardisation of sodium hydroxide solution.
Statement (II) : In this titration phenolphthalein can be used as indicator.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
solution:
A primary standard solution is a highly purified compound that is used to standardise a solution of known concentration for use in titrations and other analytical chemistry procedures.

Potassium hydrogen phthalate is primary standard for NaOH. Formula for  Potassium hydrogen phthalate is C6H5O4K. It behaves as a monobasic acid.

Phenolphthalein is used as an indicator in this titration.

The reaction is, 

Hence option A is the answer.

question: The volume of 0.1 M strong dibasic acid sufficient to neutralize 1g of a base that furnishes 0.04 mole of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math> in aqueous solution is :
solution:
For neutralisation;

⇒2×MaVa=0.04
⇒2×0.1×Va=0.04
⇒Va=0.040.2=0.2 L=200 mL

q: While titration dilute <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HCI</mi></math> solution with aqueous <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math>, which of the following will not be required ?
question: While titration dilute <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HCI</mi></math> solution with aqueous <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math>, which of the following will not be required ?
solution:
In this acid base Titrating there is no use of Bunsen burner and measuring cylinder other laboratory equipments will be required for getting the end point of titration.

These are the equipment required for titration.

  • Burette
  • Conical flask
  • Beaker
  • Pipette
  • Stirring rod
  • Small (filter) funnel
  • Burette stand and clamp (note 2)
  • White tile 
  • Indicator

question: The species given below that does <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NOT</mi></math> show disproportionation reaction is:

question:

\(\mathrm{F}_2, \mathrm{Cl}_2, \mathrm{Br}_2\) and \(\mathrm{I}_2\)
Which can undergo disproportionation reactions?
solution: $\mathrm{F}_2$ do not disproportionate because fluorine do not exist in positive oxidation state however $\mathrm{Cl}_2, \mathrm{Br}_2 \& \mathrm{I}_2$ undergoes disproportionation.

subject: Chemistry
q: 20 mL of calcium hydroxide was consumed when it was reacted with 10 mL of unknown solution of H2SO4. Also 20 mL standard solution of 0.5 M HCl containing 2 drops of phenolphthalein was titrated with calcium hydroxide, the mixture showed pink colour when burette displayed the value of 35.5 mL whereas the burette showed 25.5 mL initially. The concentration of H2SO4 is _______M. (Nearest integer)
question: 20 mL of calcium hydroxide was consumed when it was reacted with 10 mL of unknown solution of H2SO4. Also 20 mL standard solution of 0.5 M HCl containing 2 drops of phenolphthalein was titrated with calcium hydroxide, the mixture showed pink colour when burette displayed the value of 35.5 mL whereas the burette showed 25.5 mL initially. The concentration of H2SO4 is _______M. (Nearest integer)
solution:
Titration principle is N1V1=N2V2

N = Normality

V = Volume of solution

M1V1n1=M2V2n2

M = molarity

n = Valence factor of acid or base

As pink colour is obtained on consumption of 10 ml of Ca(OH)2, we have

20×0.5×1=10×M×2

Molarity of Ca(OH)2=1020=12M

Also, moles of Ca(OH)2=mm of H2SO4

12×20=10×M

Molarity of H2SO4=1M

subject: Chemistry
q: Calomel $\left(\mathrm{Hg}_2 \mathrm{Cl}_2\right)$ on reaction with ammonium hydroxide gives
question: Calomel $\left(\mathrm{Hg}_2 \mathrm{Cl}_2\right)$ on reaction with ammonium hydroxide gives
solution: $\mathrm{Hg} \mathrm{Cl}+2 \mathrm{NHOH} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hg}+\mathrm{Hg}\left(\mathrm{NH}_2\right) \mathrm{Cl}+\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl}+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
For the reaction given below:

CoCl3. xNH3+AgNO3aq→

If two equivalents of AgCl precipitate out, then the value of x will be _______
question:
For the reaction given below:

CoCl3. xNH3+AgNO3aq→

If two equivalents of AgCl precipitate out, then the value of x will be _______
solution:
CoCl3.xNH3+AgNO3→AgCl↓2 mol

For 2 moles of AgCl precipitate out,only two chloride ions should be present out side the coordination sphere that is in the ionization sphere.

Hence, the formula of the complex should be CoNH35ClCl2

CoNH35ClCl2+AgNO3→AgCl↓2 mol

So, the value of x should be 5.

x=5.

subject: Chemistry
q: The species which does not undergo disproportionation reaction is :
question: The species which does not undergo disproportionation reaction is :
solution:
$\mathrm{ClO}_4^{-} \rightarrow x+\{(-2) \times 4\}=-1 \Rightarrow x=+7$

Chlorine is in its maximum oxidation state, so disproportionation not possible in $\mathrm{ClO}_4^{-}$.
